writing.task1: The graph shows how much government debt made up the GDP of Japan, the United States, Italy, and Germany from 2000 to 2025. Overall, Japan had the highest debt during the whole period, while Germany had the lowest. Most countries saw their debt increase, except Germany which managed to reduce it. In 2000, Japan’s debt was already around 140% of its GDP and kept rising to about 260% by 2025. The United States started with about 55% and went up to more than 120%. Italy stayed near 100% until 2010, then slowly increased to around 150% in 2025. Germany’s debt rose a little until 2010, but after that it went down to below 70% by 2025. In general, Japan and the U.S. had the biggest growth in debt, while Germany was the only country that succeeded in cutting it down
writing.task2: Some people think globalization mainly helps rich countries and big companies, while others believe it brings benefits to many people and helps the world develop. On one hand, globalization allows powerful nations and corporations to expand their influence. Large companies often move their factories to poorer countries where labor is cheap, which can lead to worker exploitation and low wages. In some cases, factories harm the environment by polluting air and water to cut costs. Meanwhile, rich countries gain most of the profit through trade and technology, leaving poorer nations dependent and less able to grow on their own. On the other hand, globalization has also improved many lives. It has created millions of jobs in developing countries, allowing people to earn money and escape poverty. It also encourages the exchange of ideas, culture, and technology between countries. This has helped improve education, medicine, and innovation around the world. In my opinion, globalization is not completely good or bad. It has brought real progress, but the benefits are not shared equally. Governments and international organizations should make sure that companies follow fair labor laws and protect the environment, so that globalization can support both people and the planet

sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation about international trade agreement
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
Good morning. This is the International English Language Testing System, IELTS. You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only. The test is in four parts. At the end of the test you will be given ten minutes to transfer your answers to an answer sheet.

Now turn to Part 1. You will hear a conversation between a trade negotiator and an economic advisor about a proposed international agreement. Look at questions 1 to 10. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Dr. Reynolds, thank you for joining me to discuss the Trans-Pacific Economic Partnership. We're facing some complex challenges regarding tariff harmonization and intellectual property provisions.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The pleasure is mine, Ambassador Chen. The current draft proposes a 15-year tariff reduction schedule, but I'm concerned about the asymmetric impact on developing economies within the partnership.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I share your concern. Our economic modeling suggests that while developed nations would see a 3.2% GDP boost over the decade, some developing economies might experience initial contraction in their manufacturing sectors.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Precisely. We need to incorporate more robust safeguard mechanisms and technical assistance provisions. The dispute resolution framework also requires strengthening - particularly regarding environmental and labor standards.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I propose we establish a multilateral oversight committee with independent arbitration authority. Additionally, we should consider graduated implementation timelines based on economic development indicators.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That's a constructive approach. We could use the Human Development Index and per capita GDP thresholds to determine implementation schedules. This would address the equity concerns while maintaining economic efficiency.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Regarding intellectual property, the current 20-year patent protection period seems excessive for pharmaceutical products. This could limit access to essential medicines in lower-income member states.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I recommend a tiered approach: 15 years for developed economies, 12 for emerging markets, and 8 for least developed countries. We could also include compulsory licensing provisions for public health emergencies.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That seems balanced. The digital trade chapter also needs attention - data localization requirements could hinder cross-border e-commerce, but we must respect data sovereignty concerns.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: We could model the EU's adequacy decisions framework, allowing data flows to countries with equivalent privacy protections. This would facilitate digital trade while maintaining privacy standards.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of discussion?
questions.translation: Chủ đề thảo luận chính là gì?

questions.questionText: What is the proposed tariff reduction schedule?
questions.translation: Lịch trình giảm thuế được đề xuất là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: What GDP boost is predicted for developed nations?
questions.translation: Mức tăng GDP dự đoán cho các nước phát triển là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: Which sectors might experience contraction in developing economies?
questions.translation: Những lĩnh vực nào có thể trải qua sự thu hẹp ở các nền kinh tế đang phát triển?

questions.questionText: What is proposed to address equity concerns?
questions.translation: Điều gì được đề xuất để giải quyết mối quan tâm về công bằng?

questions.questionText: Which indicators are suggested for implementation schedules?
options: A) HDI and GDP per capita
questions.translation: Những chỉ số nào được đề xuất cho lịch trình triển khai?

questions.questionText: What concern is raised about intellectual property?
questions.translation: Mối quan tâm nào được nêu lên về sở hữu trí tuệ?

questions.questionText: What patent period is suggested for least developed countries?
questions.translation: Thời hạn bằng sáng chế nào được đề xuất cho các nước kém phát triển nhất?

questions.questionText: What issue is mentioned regarding digital trade?
questions.translation: Vấn đề nào được đề cập liên quan đến thương mại kỹ thuật số?

questions.questionText: Which framework is suggested for data flows?
questions.translation: Khuôn khổ nào được đề xuất cho luồng dữ liệu?

parts.title: Part 2: Monologue about monetary policy frameworks
parts.instructions: Listen to the monologue and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a lecture on modern monetary policy frameworks. Look at questions 11 to 20. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Contemporary central banking has evolved significantly from the inflation-targeting paradigm that dominated the late 20th century. We now operate within a more flexible framework that incorporates financial stability considerations alongside price stability objectives. The traditional 2% inflation target remains relevant but is now complemented by assessments of asset price bubbles, credit growth, and systemic risk. Quantitative easing, once considered unconventional, has become a standard tool during liquidity crises. However, the normalization of central bank balance sheets presents complex challenges, particularly regarding market expectations and forward guidance. The integration of climate risk into monetary policy frameworks represents another paradigm shift, with central banks increasingly considering environmental sustainability in their collateral frameworks and asset purchase programs. Digital currency innovations, including central bank digital currencies, are reshaping the monetary transmission mechanism while raising important questions about financial inclusion and privacy. These developments necessitate a more holistic approach to monetary policy that acknowledges the interconnectedness of price stability, financial stability, and sustainable economic growth.

questions.questionText: What paradigm dominated the late 20th century?
questions.translation: Mô hình nào thống trị cuối thế kỷ 20?

questions.questionText: What is incorporated alongside price stability?
questions.translation: Cái gì được kết hợp cùng với ổn định giá cả?

questions.questionText: What remains the traditional inflation target?
questions.translation: Mục tiêu lạm phát truyền thống là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: What has become a standard tool during crises?
questions.translation: Cái gì đã trở thành công cụ tiêu chuẩn trong khủng hoảng?

questions.questionText: What presents complex challenges during normalization?
questions.translation: Cái gì đặt ra thách thức phức tạp trong quá trình bình thường hóa?

questions.questionText: What is being integrated into monetary policy?
questions.translation: Cái gì đang được tích hợp vào chính sách tiền tệ?

questions.questionText: Where are environmental considerations applied?
questions.translation: Các cân nhắc môi trường được áp dụng ở đâu?

questions.questionText: What is reshaping the monetary transmission mechanism?
questions.translation: Cái gì đang định hình lại cơ chế truyền tải tiền tệ?

questions.questionText: What questions do digital currencies raise?
questions.translation: Tiền tệ kỹ thuật số đặt ra những câu hỏi gì?

questions.questionText: What approach is now necessary?
questions.translation: Phương pháp tiếp cận nào bây giờ là cần thiết?

parts.title: Part 3: Discussion about behavioral economics applications
parts.instructions: Listen to the discussion and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear a discussion between a behavioral economist and a policy analyst about applying behavioral insights to public policy. Look at questions 21 to 30. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The application of behavioral economics to public policy has demonstrated remarkable potential for improving policy outcomes. Nudge theory, in particular, has shown how subtle changes in choice architecture can significantly influence behavior without restricting options.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Indeed, the success of automatic enrollment in retirement savings plans is a compelling example. However, I'm concerned about the ethical dimensions - aren't we essentially manipulating citizens' choices, even if benevolently?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That's a valid concern. The key ethical principle should be transparency and alignment with people's long-term interests. When we design opt-out systems for organ donation or green energy, we're helping people overcome present bias and achieve what they genuinely want in the long run.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: But who defines those long-term interests? There's a risk of technocratic paternalism. We need robust oversight mechanisms and public deliberation about which behavioral interventions are legitimate.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I completely agree. That's why behavioral insights teams should operate with clear ethical frameworks and independent review. The focus should be on helping people make better decisions according to their own values, not imposing external values.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Another challenge is scalability. While nudges work well in specific contexts, can they address systemic issues like inequality or climate change? Don't we still need traditional regulatory approaches for major structural problems?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Behavioral interventions complement rather than replace traditional policy tools. For complex challenges like climate change, we need carbon pricing and regulations alongside behavioral strategies that encourage sustainable choices. It's about creating a comprehensive policy ecosystem.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The evidence from tax compliance is interesting. Simplifying forms and using social norms increased compliance more effectively than increasing enforcement. This suggests behavioral approaches can enhance traditional governance mechanisms.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Exactly. And with digital governance, we have new opportunities for personalized interventions. But this raises important questions about data privacy and algorithmic fairness that we must address carefully.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: It seems the future lies in integrating behavioral insights with democratic governance, ensuring these powerful tools serve public interests while respecting individual autonomy and diversity of preferences.

questions.questionText: What has demonstrated potential for improving policy?
questions.translation: Cái gì đã chứng minh tiềm năng cải thiện chính sách?

questions.questionText: What theory is mentioned as influential?
questions.translation: Lý thuyết nào được đề cập là có ảnh hưởng?

questions.questionText: What successful example is given?
questions.translation: Ví dụ thành công nào được đưa ra?

questions.questionText: What ethical concern is raised?
questions.translation: Mối quan tâm đạo đức nào được nêu lên?

questions.questionText: What key ethical principle is suggested?
questions.translation: Nguyên tắc đạo đức chính nào được đề xuất?

questions.questionText: What risk does the policy analyst mention?
questions.translation: Nhà phân tích chính sách đề cập đến rủi ro gì?

questions.questionText: What challenge is mentioned about scalability?
questions.translation: Thách thức nào được đề cập về khả năng mở rộng quy mô?

questions.questionText: How should behavioral interventions relate to traditional tools?
questions.translation: Các can thiệp hành vi nên liên quan như thế nào với các công cụ truyền thống?

questions.questionText: What increased tax compliance effectively?
questions.translation: Cái gì đã tăng cường tuân thủ thuế hiệu quả?

questions.questionText: What must be addressed with digital governance?
questions.translation: Điều gì phải được giải quyết với quản trị kỹ thuật số?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on economic complexity and development
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on economic complexity and development pathways. Look at questions 31 to 40. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The economic complexity approach represents a paradigm shift in understanding economic development. Traditional metrics like GDP per capita fail to capture the productive knowledge embedded within an economy. The Economic Complexity Index measures the diversity and sophistication of a country's export basket, revealing its accumulated productive capabilities. Countries with higher complexity scores demonstrate greater resilience to economic shocks and more sustainable growth trajectories. The concept of relatedness explains development paths: economies tend to develop new industries that are closely related to their existing capabilities. This path dependence creates both opportunities and constraints for economic diversification. Policy implications are profound: rather than picking winners, governments should identify and remove bottlenecks in the product space. Investments in education, infrastructure, and innovation systems should focus on building adjacent capabilities that enable movement toward more complex activities. The complexity framework helps explain why some resource-rich countries struggle with diversification while some resource-poor nations achieve sophisticated industrialization. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for designing effective development strategies in an increasingly knowledge-based global economy.

questions.questionText: What does the economic complexity approach represent?
questions.translation: Cách tiếp cận phức tạp kinh tế đại diện cho điều gì?

questions.questionText: What do traditional metrics fail to capture?
questions.translation: Các chỉ số truyền thống không nắm bắt được điều gì?

questions.questionText: What does the Economic Complexity Index measure?
questions.translation: Chỉ số Phức tạp Kinh tế đo lường cái gì?

questions.questionText: What benefit do high-complexity countries demonstrate?
questions.translation: Lợi ích gì mà các nước có độ phức tạp cao thể hiện?

questions.questionText: What concept explains development paths?
questions.translation: Khái niệm nào giải thích con đường phát triển?

questions.questionText: What do economies tend to develop?
questions.translation: Các nền kinh tế có xu hướng phát triển cái gì?

questions.questionText: What should governments do instead of picking winners?
questions.translation: Chính phủ nên làm gì thay vì chọn người chiến thắng?

questions.questionText: What should investments focus on?
questions.translation: Đầu tư nên tập trung vào cái gì?

questions.questionText: What does the complexity framework help explain?
questions.translation: Khung phức tạp giúp giải thích điều gì?

questions.questionText: What type of economy is becoming increasingly important?
questions.translation: Loại hình kinh tế nào đang ngày càng trở nên quan trọng?

passages.title: Passage 1: The Evolution of Global Value Chains
passages.content: Global value chains (GVCs) have transformed international trade and production patterns over recent decades, creating complex networks of cross-border economic activity. The fragmentation of production processes across multiple countries has enabled firms to leverage comparative advantages at each stage of value creation. This disintegration of production has been facilitated by technological advances in communication and transportation, trade liberalization, and the reduction of coordination costs. Developing countries' participation in GVCs has provided opportunities for technological upgrading and industrial development, though the distribution of gains remains uneven. The smile curve hypothesis illustrates how value addition is concentrated in pre-production (R&D, design) and post-production (marketing, services) activities, while manufacturing captures relatively lower value. Recent trends include the digitalization of GVCs, the rise of servicification, and growing attention to sustainability concerns. Geopolitical tensions and pandemic disruptions have highlighted vulnerabilities in highly optimized GVCs, prompting reconsideration of resilience versus efficiency trade-offs. The future evolution of GVCs will likely involve greater regionalization, digital integration, and reconfiguration to address both economic and non-economic objectives including security, sustainability, and equity.

questions.questionText: What have global value chains transformed?
What has enabled production fragmentation?
What opportunities has GVC participation provided?
What does the smile curve illustrate?
Where is value concentrated according to the smile curve?
What recent trend is mentioned?
What have pandemic disruptions highlighted?
What trade-off is being reconsidered?
What future evolution is predicted?
What non-economic objectives are mentioned?

passages.title: Passage 2: Modern Monetary Theory and Policy Debates
passages.content: Modern Monetary Theory (MMT) has sparked intense debate among economists and policymakers by challenging conventional understandings of fiscal and monetary policy constraints. MMT proponents argue that countries issuing sovereign currency in a floating exchange rate system face no financial constraints on spending, though they remain subject to real resource constraints. According to this framework, the primary risk of excessive government spending is inflation rather than insolvency. This perspective reinterprets the role of taxes, suggesting they primarily function to control inflation and create demand for currency rather than to fund government expenditure. Critics contend that MMT underestimates inflation risks and the practical political economy challenges of implementing its prescriptions. They argue that the theory fails to adequately account for exchange rate volatility, capital flight, and the confidence mechanisms underlying modern fiat currencies. The MMT debate has highlighted fundamental questions about the nature of money, the relationship between fiscal and monetary authorities, and the appropriate policy responses to secular stagnation and climate change. While mainstream economics remains skeptical of MMT's core propositions, the theory has influenced discussions about the scope for expansionary fiscal policy in low-inflation environments and the potential for direct central bank financing of public investment in extraordinary circumstances.

questions.questionText: What has MMT challenged?
What constraints do currency-issuing countries face according to MMT?
What is the primary risk of excessive spending?
How does MMT reinterpret taxes?
What do critics say MMT underestimates?
What practical challenges do critics mention?
What does MMT fail to account for according to critics?
What fundamental questions has the debate highlighted?
options: A) About the nature of money

questions.questionText: How does mainstream economics view MMT?
What has MMT influenced discussions about?

passages.title: Passage 3: The Economics of Innovation and Technological Change
passages.content: The economics of innovation represents a critical frontier in understanding long-term economic growth and competitiveness. Traditional growth models emphasized capital accumulation and labor inputs, while contemporary frameworks recognize knowledge creation and technological progress as fundamental drivers of productivity enhancement. The concept of endogenous growth theory formalizes how investments in human capital, innovation, and knowledge contribute to economic growth, challenging the assumption of diminishing returns to capital. Innovation exhibits distinctive economic characteristics: it involves significant uncertainty, generates knowledge spillovers, and often requires complementary investments. These features create market failures that justify public intervention through intellectual property rights, research subsidies, and innovation ecosystems. The digital transformation has accelerated innovation dynamics while introducing new challenges related to data governance, platform economics, and the distribution of innovation rents. Recent research emphasizes the importance of innovation systems that connect universities, firms, and government institutions in knowledge creation and diffusion. The geography of innovation has become increasingly important, with innovation clusters demonstrating agglomeration economies and knowledge networks. Addressing grand challenges like climate change and public health requires mission-oriented innovation policies that coordinate public and private efforts toward specific technological goals. Understanding the institutional and policy conditions that foster innovation while ensuring broad distribution of its benefits remains a central challenge in innovation economics.

questions.questionText: What do contemporary growth frameworks recognize?
What does endogenous growth theory challenge?
What distinctive characteristic does innovation exhibit?
What do these features create?
What justifies public intervention?
What has the digital transformation accelerated?
What do innovation systems connect?
What demonstrates agglomeration economies?
What do mission-oriented policies coordinate?
What remains a central challenge?
options: A) Fostering innovation and benefit distribution

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The graph below shows government debt as a percentage of GDP for selected advanced economies between 2000 and 2025 (projected). Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a line graph showing economic data and asked to describe the information in your own words.

sampleData.title: Government Debt as Percentage of GDP (2000-2025)

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some economists argue that globalization has primarily benefited wealthy nations and corporations at the expense of workers and the environment, while others contend that it has lifted millions out of poverty and spurred technological progress. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with contrasting views on globalization and asked to discuss them.
keyPoints: Arguments about globalization benefiting wealthy interests

questions.questionText: Had the central bank _____ interest rates earlier, the inflation surge might have been contained.
questions.translation: Nếu ngân hàng trung ương đã tăng lãi suất sớm hơn, đợt tăng lạm phát có thể đã được kiềm chế.

questions.questionText: Not until the financial crisis _____ the vulnerabilities in the global banking system.
questions.translation: Mãi đến cuộc khủng hoảng tài chính các nhà quản lý mới hiểu được những điểm dễ bị tổn thương trong hệ thống ngân hàng toàn cầu.

questions.questionText: The trade agreement, _____ provisions cover digital commerce, represents a new generation of economic partnerships.
questions.translation: Hiệp định thương mại, mà các điều khoản của nó bao gồm thương mại kỹ thuật số, đại diện cho một thế hệ mới của các quan hệ đối tác kinh tế.

questions.questionText: _____ the economic headwinds, emerging markets demonstrated remarkable resilience.
questions.translation: Bất chấp những cơn gió ngược kinh tế, các thị trường mới nổi thể hiện khả năng phục hồi đáng chú ý.

questions.questionText: The IMF recommended that the government _____ its fiscal stimulus measures.
questions.translation: IMF khuyến nghị rằng chính phủ duy trì các biện pháp kích thích tài khóa của mình.

questions.questionText: So complex _____ that few economists predicted its full impact.
options: C) the financial system had been
D] had the financial system been
questions.translation: Hệ thống tài chính phức tạp đến mức ít nhà kinh tế nào dự đoán được tác động đầy đủ của nó.

questions.questionText: The economic reforms, _____ implemented gradually, have yielded significant productivity gains.
questions.translation: Các cải cách kinh tế, đã được thực hiện dần dần, đã mang lại những lợi ích năng suất đáng kể.

questions.questionText: _____ the central bank's intervention, currency volatility persisted.
questions.translation: Bất chấp sự can thiệp của ngân hàng trung ương, sự biến động tiền tệ vẫn tiếp diễn.

questions.questionText: The corporation is one _____ global supply chain spans over fifty countries.
questions.translation: Tập đoàn là một tập đoàn mà chuỗi cung ứng toàn cầu của nó trải rộng trên năm mươi quốc gia.

questions.questionText: _____ was the economic recovery that unemployment fell to record lows.
questions.translation: Sự phục hồi kinh tế mạnh mẽ đến mức tỷ lệ thất nghiệp giảm xuống mức thấp kỷ lục.

questions.questionText: The policy committee, all of _____ had extensive economic training, reached a unanimous decision.
questions.translation: Ủy ban chính sách, tất cả đều có đào tạo kinh tế chuyên sâu, đã đưa ra quyết định nhất trí.

questions.questionText: Little _____ the profound transformation that digitalization would bring to global commerce.
questions.translation: Các nhà kinh tế hầu như không dự đoán được sự chuyển đổi sâu sắc mà số hóa sẽ mang lại cho thương mại toàn cầu.

questions.questionText: The market reaction was _____ severe that trading had to be temporarily suspended.
questions.translation: Phản ứng thị trường nghiêm trọng đến mức giao dịch phải tạm thời bị đình chỉ.

questions.questionText: _____ the coordinated policy response, the financial system stabilized.
questions.explanation: Expression for positive cause and effect
questions.translation: Nhờ phản ứng chính sách phối hợp, hệ thống tài chính đã ổn định.

questions.questionText: The board insisted that the merger _____ completed by the quarter's end.
questions.translation: Hội đồng quản trị nhấn mạnh rằng việc sáp nhập phải được hoàn thành trước cuối quý.

questions.questionText: No sooner _____ the economic data released than markets reacted violently.
questions.translation: Ngay sau khi dữ liệu kinh tế được công bố thì thị trường phản ứng dữ dội.

questions.questionText: The fiscal stimulus package, _____ on Keynesian principles, aimed to boost aggregate demand.
questions.translation: Gói kích thích tài khóa, dựa trên các nguyên tắc Keynes, nhằm mục đích thúc đẩy tổng cầu.

questions.questionText: Such _____ the complexity of modern finance that specialized regulation became necessary.
questions.translation: Sự phức tạp của tài chính hiện đại lớn đến mức quy định chuyên ngành trở nên cần thiết.

questions.questionText: The economic indicators were _____ promising that investors regained confidence.
questions.translation: Các chỉ số kinh tế hứa hẹn đến mức các nhà đầu tư lấy lại sự tự tin.

questions.questionText: _____ all the economic uncertainties, global trade continued to expand.
questions.translation: Bất chấp tất cả những bất  định kinh tế, thương mại toàn cầu tiếp tục mở rộng.

metadata.author: IELTS Practice System
metadata.notes: IELTS Level 4.4 advanced test with economics theme covering Listening (4 parts, 40 questions), Reading (3 passages, 30 questions), Writing (2 tasks), and Grammar (20 questions). Advanced difficulty with complex economic concepts and sophisticated language structures.
